questions.question: Match each component of emotion to its description:
categories: Autonomic / bodily change (sweating, heart rate up)
Behavioural disposition (fight or flight)
Conscious experience ('I feel afraid')

correct_mapping.Physiological response: Autonomic / bodily change (sweating, heart rate up)

questions.explanation: Emotion has three components: physiological response (autonomic), action tendency (behavioural), and subjective feeling (conscious). Healthy emotional responses integrate all three; in schizophrenia they dissociate.

questions.question: Match each historical theory of emotion to its core claim:
categories: Bodily feedback causes the emotion (stimulus → body → feeling)
Thalamus relays simultaneously to cortex (feeling) and hypothalamus (body)
Bodily arousal plus cognitive label produces the emotion
Emotion arises from a medial-wall forebrain ring (cingulate, hippocampus, hypothalamus)

correct_mapping.James-Lange: Bodily feedback causes the emotion (stimulus → body → feeling)
correct_mapping.Cannon-Bard: Thalamus relays simultaneously to cortex (feeling) and hypothalamus (body)
correct_mapping.Schachter-Singer: Bodily arousal plus cognitive label produces the emotion
correct_mapping.Papez / limbic system: Emotion arises from a medial-wall forebrain ring (cingulate, hippocampus, hypothalamus)

questions.explanation: James-Lange: peripheral feedback drives emotion. Cannon-Bard (diencephalic): parallel thalamic relay. Schachter-Singer: two-factor (arousal + label). Papez/MacLean: limbic-system theory; criticized for lacking strict anatomical coherence.

questions.question: Match each dimensional / process theory to its key feature:
categories: Two axes meeting at a neutral endpoint; arousal and valence tightly coupled
Orthogonal arousal × valence axes; emotions arranged around a circle
Emotions as fluid combinations of appraisals (novelty, pleasantness, goals, coping)

correct_mapping.Vector model: Two axes meeting at a neutral endpoint; arousal and valence tightly coupled
correct_mapping.Circumplex model (Russell): Orthogonal arousal × valence axes; emotions arranged around a circle
correct_mapping.Component-process theory: Emotions as fluid combinations of appraisals (novelty, pleasantness, goals, coping)

questions.explanation: Vector model couples arousal and valence ('boomerang'). Russell's circumplex treats them as orthogonal, allowing low-arousal high-valence states (calm/content). Component-process theories treat emotions as fluid — but the prof noted they are not well-studied with neuroscience methods.

questions.question: Match each amygdala subnucleus / structure to its role in fear conditioning:
categories: Sensory input from thalamus and cortex
Output to hypothalamus and brainstem for autonomic / behavioural fear
Inhibitory gating, mediates vmPFC-driven extinction
Contextual fear conditioning (fear tied to place)

correct_mapping.Lateral amygdala: Sensory input from thalamus and cortex
correct_mapping.Central amygdala: Output to hypothalamus and brainstem for autonomic / behavioural fear
correct_mapping.Hippocampus: Contextual fear conditioning (fear tied to place)

questions.explanation: Acquisition pathway: sensory input → thalamus → lateral → basal → central amygdala → hypothalamus / brainstem → fear output. The vmPFC inhibits via intercalated cells during extinction. The hippocampus handles place-based contextual fear.

questions.question: Match each fear-output measure to what it indexes:
categories: Sweating measure of sympathetic arousal
Exaggerated eyeblink to a sudden stimulus during a fear state
Autonomic / arousal change visible in the eye

correct_mapping.Fear-potentiated startle: Exaggerated eyeblink to a sudden stimulus during a fear state
correct_mapping.Pupil dilation: Autonomic / arousal change visible in the eye

questions.explanation: These three autonomic indices are the standard outputs of fear conditioning: SCR (sympathetic sweat), fear-potentiated startle (exaggerated eyeblink during fear), and pupil dilation (sympathetic eye response).

questions.question: Match each Iowa Gambling Task profile to its description:
categories: Shift toward C & D and develop anticipatory SCRs at risky decks
Keep choosing A & B and produce no anticipatory SCRs
Still produce SCRs (signal awareness) but choose risky decks anyway

correct_mapping.Healthy controls: Shift toward C & D and develop anticipatory SCRs at risky decks
correct_mapping.vmPFC-damaged patients (e.g. Elliot): Keep choosing A & B and produce no anticipatory SCRs
correct_mapping.Healthy risk-takers: Still produce SCRs (signal awareness) but choose risky decks anyway

questions.explanation: Controls develop anticipatory SCRs and shift to advantageous decks. vmPFC patients (like Damasio's Elliot) know the rules but cannot use somatic feedback — no SCR, no shift. Healthy risk-takers still produce SCRs (the awareness signal is intact) but choose risk anyway.

questions.question: Match each emotion-regulation strategy to its description:
categories: Antecedent change of behaviour to avoid the emotional encounter (e.g. fear of flying → travel by train)
Reinterpret the elicitor's meaning to alter emotional impact after it appears

correct_mapping.Situation selection: Antecedent change of behaviour to avoid the emotional encounter (e.g. fear of flying → travel by train)
correct_mapping.Cognitive reappraisal: Reinterpret the elicitor's meaning to alter emotional impact after it appears

questions.explanation: Situation selection prevents the encounter; cognitive reappraisal reinterprets it after the fact. Reappraisal upregulates dlPFC/parietal, downregulates the amygdala, and reduces subjective negative affect — operating at all three levels of emotion.

questions.question: Match each mentalizing-network region to its function:
categories: Inferences about enduring traits
Transient context-based inferences; body location
Biological motion and gaze processing
Self-projection into past / future
questions.explanation: The mentalizing network: mPFC for trait inferences, TPJ for transient context (also implicated in body location and out-of-body experiences), STS for biological motion/gaze, and precuneus/PCC for self-projection across time.

questions.question: Match each empathy-related concept to its definition:
categories: Capacity to resonate with another's emotional state — sharing the feeling
Understanding another's emotional state without sharing it — concern, pity
Excessive arousal at another's distress, leading to self-focused coping rather than helping

correct_mapping.Empathy: Capacity to resonate with another's emotional state — sharing the feeling
correct_mapping.Sympathy: Understanding another's emotional state without sharing it — concern, pity
correct_mapping.Personal distress: Excessive arousal at another's distress, leading to self-focused coping rather than helping

questions.explanation: Empathy = shared feeling. Sympathy = understanding without sharing. Personal distress = excessive contagion that leads to self-focused coping rather than other-oriented helping.

questions.question: Match each component of Decety's 4-component empathy model to its primary brain regions:
categories: Somatosensory cortex, insula, ACC, limbic system
Inferior parietal cortex, PFC, insula
Medial and dorsolateral PFC
ACC, lateral and ventromedial PFC

correct_mapping.Emotion sharing: Somatosensory cortex, insula, ACC, limbic system

questions.explanation: Decety's model: emotion sharing is more automatic and limbic; self-awareness, mental flexibility, and emotion regulation are more cognitive. The PFC is a hub appearing in three of the four components.
